tools.title: nikto
tools.purpose: A classic web server scanner: sends thousands of requests and looks for dangerous files, default configs, outdated software and known holes. A fast initial audit of an HTTP/HTTPS service.

flags.desc: Target: URL, host or IP (can be a file with a list of hosts)
Scan port(s) (default 80); can be a comma-separated list
Force HTTPS/SSL for all requests
Disallow SSL, work only over plain HTTP
Host header value (virtual host on a single IP)
Path prefix added to all requests
Do not perform DNS resolution of the hostname
Send requests through a proxy (handy for capturing traffic in Burp)

Which check classes to enable: 1 interesting files, 2 default/misconfig, 3 info disclosure, 4 injections (XSS), 5 LFI in web-root, 6 DoS, 7 remote file read, 8 command execution, 9 SQLi, 0 file upload, a authorization bypass, b software identification, c remote source inclusion, x inversion (exclude the specified)
Which plugins to run (default @@DEFAULT); list separated by ;
Show all available plugins and databases without scanning anything
Guess names: 1 files in all directories, 2 password file names, 3 users via Apache UserDir, 4 users via cgiwrap, 5 subdomains, 6 directories by wordlist
Extra data for -mutate (for example, a wordlist for directory guessing)
HTTP Basic authentication credentials (login:password)

IDS evasion techniques (libwhisker): 1 random URI encoding, 2 directory self-reference, 3 premature URL ending, 4 long random string, 5 fake parameter, 6 TAB as separator, 7 URL case change, 8 windows slash, A carriage return as separator, B byte 0x0b as separator
Spoof the User-Agent string
Pause in seconds between each request (reduce load/noise)
Request timeout in seconds (default 10)
Maximum scan time for a single host
Save the result to a file
Report format for -output: csv, htm, txt, xml, msf, json
What to show on screen: 1 redirects, 2 cookies, 3 all 200/OK responses, 4 those requiring auth, D debug, E HTTP errors, P progress, V verbose output
Save positive server responses to the specified directory
Disable the 404 page check (faster but more false positives)
Non-interactive mode - ask no questions during the scan
Update the plugin and signature databases
Check the databases for syntax errors
Show the nikto and database version
Show help on the options

recipes.note: Basic host scan with all default checks
Scan an HTTPS site on port 443
Only interesting files/misconfig/info/software, report in HTML
Route traffic through Burp for analysis

tools.notes: A noisy tool: for bug bounty, align it with the program rules. -Tuning x inverts the selection (excludes the specified classes).

tools.title: whatweb
tools.purpose: Fingerprints a web site: identifies CMS, frameworks, servers, libraries, analytics, versions and over 1800 technologies by signatures. A fast way to understand what a site is built on.

flags.desc: Read a list of targets from a file (--input-file)
Aggression level: 1 stealthy (1 request), 3 aggressive (extra requests on a match), 4 heavy (many requests, all aggressive plugins)
Spoof the User-Agent (--user-agent)
Add an arbitrary HTTP header (--header)
Pass cookies (--cookie), format name=value; name2=value2
HTTP Basic authentication (--user), format login:password
Behavior on redirects: never, http-only, meta-only, same-site, always
Use an HTTP proxy (default port 8080)
Login and password for the proxy
Run only the selected plugins (--plugins), supports +/-
Show the list of all available plugins (--list-plugins)
Detailed plugin info with a keyword search (--info-plugins)
Search plugins by keyword
Number of threads (--max-threads, default 25)
Connection establishment timeout in seconds
Response read timeout in seconds
Pause between connections in seconds (useful with 1 thread)
Verbose output with plugin descriptions (--verbose); twice - debug
Quiet mode - do not print the brief log to stdout (--quiet)
Output color control: never, always, auto
Hide error messages
Log to a brief single-line grep format
Log to a human-readable format
Log in JSON format
Log in XML format

recipes.note: Quick fingerprint of a single site
Aggressive mode with verbose version output
Mass scan of a list with a JSON log

tools.notes: Aggression levels 3 and 4 send more requests - on bug bounty mind the rate limit.

tools.title: wafw00f
tools.subcategory: WAF detection in front of the target
tools.purpose: Determines whether a web firewall (WAF) sits in front of a site and which one exactly - Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS WAF, Imperva and about 150 more products. Helps understand why payloads are blocked and which bypass to look for.

flags.desc: Read targets from a file (--input), format csv/json/text
Find ALL matched WAFs, do not stop at the first (--findall)
Do not follow 3xx redirects (--noredirect)
Check only one specific WAF (--test); see the names in -l
Show the list of all WAFs it can detect (--list)
Use an HTTP proxy for requests (--proxy)
File with custom headers on top of the standard ones (--headers)
Request timeout in seconds (--timeout)
Verbose output; repeating -v increases detail (--verbose)
Save output to a file; format by extension csv/json/text (--output)
Force output format: csv, json, text (--format)
Disable ANSI colors in the output
Show the wafw00f version (--version)

recipes.note: Check which WAF protects the site
Find all WAFs (there can be several layers)
Mass check of a list with JSON output

tools.notes: Run it first thing: knowing the WAF defines the filter-bypass strategy.

tools.title: gospider
tools.purpose: A fast Go spider: recursively walks a site, extracts links, forms, endpoints and URLs from JS files, robots.txt and the sitemap. Can also pull URLs from external sources (archive.org, CommonCrawl). A foundation for collecting attack surface.

flags.desc: A single site to crawl (--site)
File with a list of sites (--sites)
Folder to save the results (--output)
Find links inside JS files via LinkFinder (on by default)
Try to crawl sitemap.xml
Try to crawl robots.txt (on by default)
Pull URLs from third-party sources: Archive.org, CommonCrawl, VirusTotal, AlienVault (--other-source)
Include subdomains from third-party sources (--include-subs, together with -a)
Also crawl/request URLs from third-party sources (--include-other-source)
Include subdomains while crawling
Crawl recursion depth; 0 = unlimited (--depth, default 1)
Regex: crawl only matching URLs
Regex: exclude matching URLs
Allowed domain to crawl
Filter by response length (--filter-length)
Show response length (--length)
User-Agent: web (random desktop), mobi or a custom string (--user-agent)
Add a header; the flag can be repeated (--header)
Pass cookies
Load headers and cookies from a raw Burp HTTP request
Proxy (--proxy)
Do not follow redirects
Number of threads - sites in parallel (--threads, default 1)
Max concurrent requests to a single domain (--concurrent, default 5)
Pause in seconds before a new request (--delay)
Extra random seconds added to --delay (--random-delay)
Request timeout in seconds (--timeout, default 10)
Output in JSON format
Quiet mode - show only found URLs (--quiet)
Verbose output (--verbose)
Debug mode
HTML content only, everything else disabled (--base)

recipes.note: Basic crawl of a single site
Deep crawl with subdomains and parallelism
With third-party sources (archive/CC) and JSON output
Crawl with headers/cookies from a captured Burp request

tools.title: hakrawler
tools.purpose: A simple fast Go spider for collecting URLs and endpoints. Reads targets from STDIN and fits well into pipelines (subfinder | httpx | hakrawler). Minimum settings - maximum speed.

flags.desc: Targets are read from STDIN line by line: echo {TARGET} | hakrawler or cat urls.txt | hakrawler
Crawl depth (default 2)
Include subdomains while crawling
Crawl only within the path (scope restriction)
Do not follow HTTP redirects
Custom headers; multiple are separated by a double ;;
Disable TLS certificate verification
Proxy URL
Max time to crawl a single URL in seconds (-1 = no limit)
Page size limit in KB (-1 = no limit)
Number of threads (default 8)
Show only unique URLs
Output in JSON format
Show the source of each URL (href, form, script)
Show on which page the URL was found

recipes.note: Basic crawl of a single target via stdin
Deeper, with subdomains, unique URLs only
Through Burp and without TLS verification

tools.notes: The target is ALWAYS fed via stdin (echo/cat); there is no separate URL flag.

tools.title: paramspider
tools.subcategory: Collecting parameterized URLs from the archive
tools.purpose: Pulls from the Wayback Machine (web.archive.org) all historical URLs of a domain that have GET parameters and replaces the parameter values with the FUZZ placeholder. A ready list of targets for fuzzing XSS/SQLi/LFI and the like.
tools.install: git clone https://github.com/devanshbatham/ParamSpider && cd ParamSpider && pip install .

flags.desc: Target domain for collecting URLs from the Wayback Machine (--domain)
File with a list of domains - batch mode (--list)
Placeholder instead of parameter values (--placeholder, default FUZZ)
Stream found URLs straight to the terminal as they are processed (--stream)
Proxy address for requests

recipes.note: Collect parameterized URLs of a domain (result in the results/ folder)
See URLs in the terminal right away
Custom placeholder instead of FUZZ

tools.notes: In the current version the output is automatically written to the results/<domain>.txt folder - there is no separate -o flag. The --subs and --exclude flags from older versions have been removed.

tools.title: interactsh-client
tools.purpose: An open-source analog of Burp Collaborator for blind vulnerabilities. You run the client - it hands out a unique domain like xxxx.oast.pro. You put that domain into a payload (SSRF, blind XXE, blind RCE, blind SQLi). When the target server itself resolves/requests the domain, the client prints the incoming DNS/HTTP/SMTP ping with the IP, time and protocol - which proves the vulnerability fired.

flags.desc: No target is set: run the client, get a domain *.oast.pro (oast.live, oast.site, oast.online, oast.fun, oast.me) and put it into the payload
How many payload domains to generate (--number, default 1)
Which interactsh server to use; can be your own self-hosted one (--server)
Authentication token for a protected/own server (--token)
Client configuration file
Server poll interval in seconds (--poll-interval, default 5)
Session file: save/read to get the same subdomain back (--session-file)
Disable HTTP fallback on registration (--no-http-fallback)
Keep-alive interval (--keep-alive-interval)
Show only DNS interactions
Show only HTTP interactions
Show only SMTP interactions
Show only interactions matching a pattern (--match)
Hide interactions matching a pattern (--filter)
Show ASN information for the source IP
Save interactions to a file
Output in JSONL format
Verbose mode - show the full request/response
Save the generated payload domains (--payload-store)
File to store the payloads (--payload-store-file)
Update interactsh-client to the latest version (--update)
Show the tool version
Diagnostic health check (--health-check)

recipes.note: Run and get a domain *.oast.pro to insert into a payload
See full details of incoming requests (useful for debugging SSRF)
Generate 5 domains, save the payloads and interactions
Connect to your own self-hosted server

tools.notes: Workflow: launch -> you get *.oast.pro -> drop the domain into a blind vulnerability payload -> catch DNS/HTTP pings in the terminal. That is the proof it fired.

tools.title: graphw00f
tools.purpose: A GraphQL analog of wafw00f: finds the GraphQL endpoint on a target and determines which implementation exactly sits behind it (Apollo, Graphene, graphql-ruby, HyperGraphQL and others). The engine tells you which attacks and bypasses apply.
tools.install: git clone https://github.com/dolevf/graphw00f && cd graphw00f && pip install -r requirements.txt

flags.desc: Target URL together with the path to check (--target)
detect mode: find the GraphQL endpoint path by brute-forcing known paths (--detect)
fingerprint mode: determine which GraphQL engine is running (--fingerprint)
Custom wordlist of GraphQL endpoint paths for detect mode (--wordlist)
Show the list of all engines it can detect and exit (--list)
Custom User-Agent (--user-agent)
Custom header; can be specified multiple times (--header); cookies go here too
HTTP(S) proxy, format http://user:pass@host:port (--proxy)
Request timeout in seconds (--timeout, default 10)
Do not follow 3xx redirects (--noredirect)
Write the results to a CSV file (--output-file)
Show the version and exit (--version)

recipes.note: Find the endpoint and immediately determine the engine
Fingerprint an already known /graphql endpoint
Only endpoint discovery by a custom wordlist of paths
List of all detectable engines

tools.notes: Runs as python3 main.py from the repository folder. detect (-d) finds WHERE the endpoint is, fingerprint (-f) determines WHAT the engine is; they are usually used together.

tools.title: smuggler
tools.purpose: Checks an endpoint for HTTP Request Smuggling vulnerabilities (CL.TE / TE.CL desync). Sends a series of mutated requests and determines whether the frontend and backend get out of sync on body-length parsing.

flags.desc: Target URL with the endpoint (--url); or feed the URL via stdin
Specify the virtual host - the Host header (--vhost)
HTTP request method (--method, default POST)
Socket timeout in seconds (--timeout, default 5)
Mutation/payload configuration file (--configfile, default default.py)
Exit on the first vulnerability found on an endpoint (--exit_early)
Quiet mode - output only the problems found (--quiet)
Log file (--log)
Disable ANSI colors

recipes.note: Basic check of an endpoint for smuggling
Feed the URL via stdin
POST method, stop on the first finding, quiet

tools.notes: The target is fed either via -u or via stdin (echo {TARGET} | python3 smuggler.py). Mutation configs are in the config/ folder.

tools.title: crlfuzz
tools.purpose: A fast CRLF injection scanner in Go. Inserts newline control characters into URLs and headers and checks whether the HTTP response can be split (HTTP response splitting / header injection). Works on a single URL, a list or from stdin.

flags.desc: A single URL to fuzz (--url)
File with a list of URLs (--list)
URLs can be fed via stdin: cat urls.txt | crlfuzz
HTTP request method (--method, default GET)
Request body / POST data (--data)
Custom header; can be specified multiple times (--header)
Use a proxy (--proxy)
Concurrency level (--concurrent, default 25)
Quiet mode - show only vulnerable targets (--silent)
Save the results to a file (--output)
Verbose mode - show errors/details (--verbose)
Show the version (--version)

recipes.note: Check a single URL for CRLF injection
Scan a list, vulnerable only, to a file
From stdin with increased concurrency (for pipelines)
